coredata.dc:title: Ternary deep eutectic solvent modified cadmium selenide quantum dots as a selective fluorescent probe for sensing of uranyl ions in water samples
coredata.dc:creator: Sadeghi, Susan
Davami, Abdolbaset
coredata.dc:description: A single step hydrothermal approach for the synthesis of CdSe QDs using green precursor materials with the assistance of urea-thioglycolic acid-choline chloride ternary deep eutectic solvent (TDES-CdSe QDs) in an aqueous solution is reported. The fluorescent TDES-CdSe QDs probe was successfully applied to the fast and selective detection of uranyl ions. It was found that the fluorescence emission of the probe was dramatically quenched by uranyl ions, while other ions had no significant influence on the fluorescence intensity of the DES-CdSe QDs. The nanoprobe was systematically characterized by FTIR, XRD, TEM and EDX analysis. Some key factors affecting the performance of TDES-CdSe QDs probe were optimized using response surface methodology based on face centered central composite design. Under the optimal conditions, the DES-CdSe QDs probe provided a linear response to uranyl ions in the concentration range from 10 to 500 nM with a limit of detection 5.7 nM. The relative standard deviation (RSD%) of the developed method for five replicate determinations of uranyl ions at 100 nM was estimated to be 1.9%. In addition, the proposed method was successfully explored to the uranyl ions quantitation in environmentally water samples and desirable results were achieved.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Uranyl ions
Ternary deep eutectic solvent
CdSe QDs
Fluorescent probe
Face centered central composite design

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optical properties of the TDES-CdSe QDs
Analytical performance of the fluorometric probe for uranyl ions
Comparison of the proposed method with other fluorometric methods based on QDs for uranyl ions determination

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Authors are grateful to the Research Council of the University of Birjand for supporting this work.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectra of TDES-CdSe QDs and (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of TDES-CdSe QDs, DES-CdSe QDs and TGA-CdSe QDs in the absence and presence of uranyl ions. Conditions: pH 5; concentration of UO2 2+, 200 nM; sample volume, 5 mL; concentration of buffer, 10 mM; volume of QDs, 90 μL.
(A) FT-IR spectrum, (B) XRD pattern, (C) TEM image, (D) Histogram of particle size and (E) EDX spectrum of TDES-CdSe QDs probe.

(A) Normal probability plot of residuals for the response, (B) A scheme of predicted responses versus actual responses, (C) Perturbation plot for determination of uranyl ions using TDES-CdSe QDs and (D) Contour plot of interaction between pH and buffer concentration.
Effects of (A) the buffer pH, (B) the buffer concentration and (C) the TDES-CdSe QDs volume on the response of the probe.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of TDES-CdSe QDs with varying concentrations of uranyl ions (from the top to the bottom: 0, 10, 20, 30, 50, 70, 100, 150, 200, 250, 300, 400, 500, 600, 700, 800, 900, and 1000 nM), (B) Linear fitting curve of response vs. concentration of uranyl ions over a range from 10 to 500 nM, and (C) Langmuir binding isotherm for the entire range of uranyl ion concentrations. The inset shows a linear plot of Langmuir binding isotherm at lower uranyl ion concentrations. Conditions: pH 5; sample volume, 5 mL; concentration of buffer, 10 mM; volume of QDs, 90 μL.

(A) The selectivity of TDES-CdSe QDs to various metal ions (200 nM), and (B)The effect of interferences on detection of uranyl ions using the TDES-CdSe QDs probe. Conditions: pH 5; sample volume, 5 mL; concentration of buffer, 10 mM; volume of QDs, 90 μL.
Variables and their levels utilized in the face centered central composite design (FCCCD).
Design matrix and corresponding responses of the FCCCD.
Analysis of variance (ANOVA) for the FCCCD.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Test for comparing model variance with residual (error) variance.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The probability if the null hypothesis is true.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Analytical results for uranyl ions monitoring in environmental water samples by the developed method.
Comparison of the proposed method with other fluorometric methods based on QDs for uranyl ions detection.

head.ce:title: Ternary deep eutectic solvent modified cadmium selenide quantum dots as a selective fluorescent probe for sensing of uranyl ions in water samples

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, University of Birjand, P.O. Box. 97175/615, Birjand, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, University of Birjand, P.O. Box. 97175/615, Birjand, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A single step hydrothermal approach for the synthesis of CdSe QDs using green precursor materials with the assistance of urea-thioglycolic acid-choline chloride ternary deep eutectic solvent (TDES-CdSe QDs) in an aqueous solution is reported. The fluorescent TDES-CdSe QDs probe was successfully applied to the fast and selective detection of uranyl ions. It was found that the fluorescence emission of the probe was dramatically quenched by uranyl ions, while other ions had no significant influence on the fluorescence intensity of the DES-CdSe QDs. The nanoprobe was systematically characterized by FTIR, XRD, TEM and EDX analysis. Some key factors affecting the performance of TDES-CdSe QDs probe were optimized using response surface methodology based on face centered central composite design. Under the optimal conditions, the DES-CdSe QDs probe provided a linear response to uranyl ions in the concentration range from 10 to 500 nM with a limit of detection 5.7 nM. The relative standard deviation (RSD%) of the developed method for five replicate determinations of uranyl ions at 100 nM was estimated to be 1.9%. In addition, the proposed method was successfully explored to the uranyl ions quantitation in environmentally water samples and desirable results were achieved.

ce:section.ce:para: Health or environmental topic affected by abnormal amount of heavy metal ions is a global concern. Therefore, developing of selective and sensitive methods for fast detection of trace concentration of heavy metal ions in environmental samples are of great importance. Uranium is a highly chemical and radiological toxic heavy metal ion. It is widely used as fuel source of nuclear power plants, nuclear weapons, medical or industrial applications. Naturally, uranium is present in rocks, soils and waters. It exists in different oxidation state, but the most stable and soluble form with maximum bioavailability in the environment is uranyl ions (UO2 2+). It enters into ground or surface water through migration. Also, social activities such as mining and treating uranium are other reasons for presence of uranium in drinking waters and human food chain. Due to high toxicity and long half-life, uranium affects human health such as severe damage of liver, lung, skeleton and kidney. According to the United State Environmental Protection Agency (U.S.EPA), the concentration of uranium in drinking water should not exceed 130 nM [1–4]. Thus, developing an efficient and rapid method that able to monitor the trace concentration of uranyl ions in environmental samples is extremely urgent needed. Until now, various analytical techniques for quantitative monitoring of uranyl ion concentrations have been employed including inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [5], inductively coupled plasma-optical emission spectrometry (ICP-OES) [6], laser induced kinetic phosphorimetry [7], X-ray fluorescence spectroscopy [8] and surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy [9]. Although these techniques provide good sensitivity and satisfactory repeatability, but some of them require costly instruments and skilled operators and possess time consuming and tedious sample preparation. In addition, these techniques have no ability to real-time and on-site monitoring of target analyte [3]. Therefore, it is desirable to develop straightforward and low cost methods for fast detection of uranyl ions. Ultraviolet-visible (UV–Vis) spectrophotometry is a suitable alternative technique for determination of uranyl ions due to simplicity, repeatability and low cost. But this technique suffers from some drawbacks that limits it applications such as using toxic chromogenic reagents like Arsenazo (III). In addition, because of the inherent insufficient sensitivity of UV–Vis spectrophotometry and interferences of some metal ions like lanthanides and actinides, the direct determination of low concentration of uranyl ions is impossible and preconcentration step before determination is necessary. Hence, it is essential to develop simple, green and fast methods for quantification of uranyl ions. Various analytical methods including solid phase extraction (SPE) [10–12], cloud point extraction (CPE) [2], and dispersive liquid-liquid extraction (DLLME) [13] have been employed for the separation/preconcentration of uranium from aqueous samples prior to determination. In recent years, microextraction methods which selectively extract analytes from environmental samples have great attention. Environmentally friendly and highly efficient liquid-liquid microextraction methods based on supramolecular [14], and ionic liquid [15] solvents with using suitable chelating agents have been employed for selective preconcentration of uranyl ions prior to its determination by UV–Vis spectroscopy.

Recently, several types of novel nanomaterials as fluorescent probes have been introduced that exhibited considerable potential in the determination of uranyl ions. These materials are including semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) [16], carbon dots (CDs) [17], luminescent metal organic frameworks (MOFs) [18], fluorescent polymers [4] and DNAzyme based gold nanocluster or nanowire probes [19,20].

QDs as fluorescent probes have been received increasing attention over the past three decades due to unique optical and electronic properties like narrow emission bands, broad absorption spectrum, high quantum yield, tunable size-dependent emission and good photostability. On the other hand, the surface of QDs can be easily modified with various capping agents that make them sensitive to interact with some substances [21].

Lately, few papers in fluorometric detection of uranyl ions using the semiconductor QDs based nanosensors have been reported. Dutta and Kumar described a fluorecent probe based on CdS QDs modified with thiourea for the ultrasensitive determination of uranyl ions with good linear range, but some cations and anions like Pb2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, SO4 2− and CO3 2− interfered in the determination of uranyl ions [16]. Saha et al. designed a diglycolamide capped CdS/ZnS QDs probe for ultratrace monitoring of uranium through direct Foster Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET) mechanism. This probe displayed a good selectivity to uranium, however, the synthesis process was multistep and time consuming; as well as preconcentration step was performed before detection of uranium [22]. Chen et al. synthesized CdTe functionalized using mercaptopropionic acid as a fluorescent sensor for quantitative determination of uranyl ions through electron transfer mechanism. Although this sensor provided excellent sensitivity to uranyl ions, but its response to copper ions was higher than uranyl ions, so copper ions had a significant interfering effect on the detection of uranyl ions [23]. The research group of Hua also prepared a specific size of CdTe QDs for detection of uranyl ions. The synthesized probe showed excellent selectivity and sensitivity to uranyl ions. However, the synthesis process is complicated, time consuming and requires the preparation of QDs in an inert atmosphere. Additionally, selectivity of this probe vitally depended on the size of QDs [24]. Therefore, to improve the selection and sensitivity of the target analyte, it is necessary to focus on the preparation of semiconductor QDs with simple synthesis processes and non-toxic precursors.

Deep eutectic solvents (DES) have emerged as a new class of solvents greener than analogs of IL with promising applications in various fields. DESs have shown unique properties like low volatility, non-flammability, low cost, low toxicity and biodegradability. In addition, easy, and straightforward preparation involves no further purification are another advantages of these solvents. DESs are generally produced by mixing two or three components in which a self association is formed through hydrogen bonding with the melting point below each of the individual components [25,26]. Up to now, DESs have been used in synthesis of some carbon dots [27–29], but there is no report for utilizing of DESs as capping agent in producing of semiconductor QDs.

In the present study, we report an environmentally friendly and single step preparing of functionalized CdSe QDs in ternary deep eutectic solvent consisted of choline chloride, thiodiacetic acid and thioglycolic acid in molar ratio 1:1:1 in aqueous solution which is named TDES-CdSe QDs. The reaction was performed in an air atmosphere by using ascorbic acid as nontoxic reducing agent and SeO2 as inexpensive and stable selenium precursor. In this approach, no organic solvent or additive was used. These QDs were studied for their size, structure, as well as optical characteristics. It is found that the TDES capping agent for synthesizing CdSe QDs facilitated interaction with uranyl ion, so it was used for the fast analysis of uranyl ions via quenching of photoluminescence signal. To the best of our knowledge, the developed method is the first report of application of the ternary deep eutectic solvent in the synthesis of the semiconductor QDs. The significant variables that influenced the performance of the method were optimized using face centered central composite design (FCCCD). Finally, the probe was successfully applied to water samples analysis.

All chemicals used in the present study were analytical grade and applied without further purification. Cadmium chloride hemi (pentahydrate) (CdCl2·2.5H2O), selenium dioxide (SeO2), ascorbic acid (C6H8O6), choline chloride (C5H14ClNO), thioglycolic acid (C2H4O2S), thiodiacetic acid (C4H6O4S) and uranium nitrate hexahydrate (UO2(NO3)2·6H2O) were purchased from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany, http://www.merck.de). Acetic acid and sodium acetate were utilized to prepare the acetate buffer solution. The stock solution of uranyl ions (1.0 × 10−3 M) was prepared with adding appropriate amount of nitrate salt into deionized water. The stock solutions of other cations or anions were made by dissolving the suitable amount of nitrate or sodium salts, respectively, in deionized water. The working standard solutions with certain concentration were freshly prepared by diluting the stock solution with deionized water.

The fluorescence spectra were recorded on a spectrofluorometer (RF-5301-PC, Shimadzu, Japan, https://www.shimadzu.com) equipped with a xenon discharge lamp and 1 cm quartz cell. The excitation and emission slit widths were set at 5 and 10 nm, respectively. The absorption spectra were taken on ultraviolet-visible spectrophotometer (2510-PC, Shimadzu, Japan, https://www.shimadzu.com) in the wavelength range from 300 to 700 nm. These QDs were then subjected to various characterization studies, namely ultraviolet–visible absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy for optical characteristics, and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) on a FT-IR spectrophotometer (4600 FT-IR, JASCO, Japan, https://www.jasco.co.jp) with KBr pellet in the wavenumber range from 400 to 4000 cm−1for the confirmation of functional groups. The morphology and size of TDES-CdSe QDs were studied using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) on TEM instrument (EM10C TEM, Zeiss, Germany, http://www. Zeiss.com) operated at an acceleration voltage of 100 kV. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of TDES-CdSe QDs was achieved using an X-ray diffractometer (PW1730 Philips, Netherlands, https://www.philips.com) in the 2θ range from 10 to 70° through Cu Kα radiation. To identify the elemental composition of TDES-CdSe QDs, energy dispersive X-ray analysis (EDX) was performed on a scanning electron microscope (VEGA TESCAN, Brno, Czech Republic, https://www.tescan.com). The deionized water was obtained by aqua max water 370 purification system (Young-Lin, South Korea, https://eng.youngincm.com). A benchtop pH meter (BP3001, Trans instrument, Singapore https://www.transinstruments.com) equipped with combined glass electrode was employed for pH adjustment.

In order to synthesis the TDES, choline chloride (1.6 mmol), thiodiacetic acid (1.6 mmol) and thioglycolic acid (1.6 mmol) were poured in the screw capped bottle. Subsequently, the mixture was heated to 60 °C under stirring for 30 min to obtain a homogenous and colorless liquid. The DES composed from choline chloride (1.6 mmol) and thioglycolic acid (1.6 mmol) was synthesized in a similar manner.

The TDES-CdSe QDs were prepared by a one-pot synthesis in the aqueous route under atmospheric condition according to Wang et al. method with a minor modification [30]. In this method, inexpensive and ecofriendly compounds such as SeO2 and ascorbic acid as Se source and reducing agents have been used for the synthesis of TDES-CdSe QDs, respectively. In a typical synthesis process, CdCl2·2.5 H2O (1 mmol) as a cadmium source was dissolved in 50 mL of deionized water in a 100 mL round flask to form a clear solution. In the next step, the synthesized TDES in Section 2.3 was added to the cadmium solution and the mixture was stirred at room temperature. After stirring for 30 min, the pH of the mixture was adjusted to 11.8 using NaOH (1 M). Ascorbic acid (176 mg) and SeO2 (0.1 mmol) were then added to the mixture as reducing agent and Se source, respectively, under stirring and the pH was adjusted again to 11.8 using NaOH (1 M). The mixture was refluxed under ambient condition at 100 °C for 10 h. The final concentration of the synthesized TDES-CdSe QDs was calculated based on Se2−concentration of about 2 mM. We also investigated the influence of TDES molar ratio on the optical properties of TDES-CdSe QDs with changing the TDES: Cd molar ratio from 1.2 to 3.2 and fixing the Cd/Se molar ratio at 1:0.1.

The nanoparticles were repeatedly washed with ethanol and centrifuged at 3500 rpm for 5 min. The product was dried in vacuum oven at room temperature. Next, the resulting QDs were dispersed again in 50 mL deionized water and kept at 4 °C until subsequent use. The TGA-CdSe QDs and DES-CdSe QDs were prepared in a similar approach for comparison.

In a 10 mL tube, 0.5 mL of acetate buffer (0.1 M, pH 5), 90 μL of TDES-CdSe QDs and 100 μL of different concentrations of uranyl ions were added. Next, the mixture was diluted to a final volume of 5 mL with deionized water. Then, the resulting mixture was shaken for 2 min to complete the reaction. After that, the fluorescence emission spectra were recorded in the wavelength range from 350 to 700 nm, whereas the excitation wavelength was set at 380 nm. The intensity of fluorescence was measured at 560 nm. The difference in fluorescence intensities in the absence (F0) and presence (F) of uranyl ions, (ΔF = F0-F), against uranyl concentrations was used to obtain the calibration plot.

Response surface methodology (RSM) is a statistical method that offers an efficient manner for studying the influence of variables affecting the performance of a method by a limited number of experiments. In the present study, to investigate the main variables and interactions between the variables and to achieve the optimum condition of these variables, the RSM based face centered central composite design (FCCCD) was employed. Based on preliminarily experiments, it was found that three variables affect the efficiency of uranyl ions detection. These variables - including sample pH (A), buffer concentration (B) and TDES-CdSe QDs volume (C) were evaluated by FCCCD at three levels. Table 1 presents the variables and their values. The design matrix is randomly composed from 2 blocks and 20 experiments due to prevent systematic error. In this study, ΔF = F0-F (described in the Section 2.5) was selected as the analytical response. The design matrix and their responses are illustrated in Table 2 . In all experiments, the fluorescence was recorded at the maximum excitation/emission wavelengths 380/560 nm. The concentration and volume of uranyl solutions were set at 200 nM and 5 mL, respectively. The experimental design was carried out using Design Expert software (Stat-Ease, Inc., Minneapolis, USA) version 10.0.0.

To confirm the practical applicability and reliability of the suggested method, two water samples including tap water and sea water were selected and the proposed method was utilized to determine uranyl ions. The tap water was obtained from our research laboratory (University of Birjand, Birjand, Iran). The seawater was collected from Oman Sea (Chabahar, Iran). The water samples were firstly filtered using a 0.45 μm micropore membrane filter to remove the suspended particulates and stored at 4 °C before use. Finally, these samples were analyzed based on the process described in Section 2.5.

To synthesis CdSe QDs, it is important to use green and stable reagents. For this purpose, we used selenium dioxide (SeO2) as a cheap, less toxic and air stable selenium precursor compared to Se powder. Besides, ascorbic acid (C6H8O6) as an inexpensive, non-toxic and stable reducing agent is a good alternative to NaBH4 or N2H4, H2O in the synthesis of QDs CdSe [30]. This new synthesis was carried out in one step without the use of an inert atmosphere, due to chemical stability of SeO2 and ascorbic acid. TDES was also used as the new and green capping agent of CdSe QDs. Preliminary experiments revealed that among TDES-CdSe QDs with different TDES molar ratios, the TDES-CdSe QDs with a Cd: Se: TDES molar ratio of 1: 0.1: 1.6 had the best response to monitor uranyl ions and was chosen for further studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of the TDES-CdSe QDs
ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties of the TDES-CdSe QDs were examined by UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectra (Fig. 1A).The TDES-CdSe QDs shows a light-yellow color in natural light. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of TDES-CdSe QDs is depicted in Fig. 1A. The maximum absorption of the TDES-CdSe QDs appeared at 410 nm. The absorption onsets for the TDES-CdSe QDs spectrum appeared at 470 nm which obviously blue shifted compared with bulk CdSe absorption spectrum at 716 nm, representing the influence of quantum confinement [31]. The large blue-shifting in wavelength confirmed the small particle sizes of TDES-CdSe QDs.

The emission spectrum depicts a two-peak profile, one centered at about 450 nm and a broad band centered at 560 nm (Fig. 1A). The broad red shifted emission comes from both electron and hole recombinations in surface states which may be attributed to defect related emissions. The high surface area-to-volume ratio of the nanoparticles gives rise to emissive surface trap states which are difficult to be passivated and represent a broad emission peak. The first peak is sensitive to ligand which put on the surface, so it is a surface state. As observed in Fig. 1A, the first photoluminescence band of the CdSe QDs located close to the absorption thresholds so-called band-edge or ‘excitonic’ photoluminescence. This is probably a result of the strong capping of Cd2+ ions with TDES molecules (one-SH and two-COOH groups), indicating a narrow size distribution. Thus, the traps on the particle surface can be effectively removed. As a result, intermittent emissions are observed in both excitonic recombination and surface trap related processes. Accordingly, the emission centered at 450 nm can be attributed to excitonic luminescence and those over 560 nm related to the emissions from surface trap sites. A high difference between the excitation and emission wavelengths makes simple fluorescence measurements [32].

To examine the photostability of the TDES-CdSe QDs, the fluorescence intensity of the TDES-CdSe QDs at maximum emission wavelength (560 nm) was measured after illumination for 60 min (Fig. S1A). It was found that continuous radiation had no significant decreasing in fluorescence intensity. In addition, the TDES-CdSe QDs exhibited excellent optical stability in ambient conditions in a period of one month with a deviation less than 8.5% in fluorescence intensity (Fig. S1B). This feature is particularly important for treatment as a fluorescent probe compared to organic fluorophores that suffer from poor photostability.

The fluorescence emission spectra of three different CdSe QDs namely TGA-CdSe QDs, DES-CdSe QDs and TDES-CdSe QDs were assessed to further investigating the optical properties of CdSe QDs upon addition of uranyl ions. As presented in Fig. 1B, fluorescence intensities of TGA-CdSe QDs and DES-CdSe QDs were unchanged upon addition of uranyl ions (200 nM), whereas the fluorescence intensity of TDES-CdSe QDs in the presence of uranyl ions was significantly quenched. The presence of two carboxylic groups in thiodiacetic acid in interaction with uranyl ions may be a reason of high fluorescence quenching of TDES-CdSe QDs. Additionally, no wavelength shift in the emission peaks of TGA-CdSe QDs, DES-CdSe QDs or TDES-CdSe QDs were detected upon addition of uranyl ions, indicating binding of uranyl ions to the surface of quantum dots.

To ascertain the successful capping of CdSe QDs with TDES, the FT-IR spectrum of the synthesized QDs was recorded in the range of 400–4000 cm−1 (Fig. 2A). According to Fig. 2A, a broad absorption band is observed at 3364 cm−1 assigned to (OH) stretching vibration. The absorption peak at 2917 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of CH band of methyl group existing in the TDES structure. Sharp peaks at 1641 cm−1 and 1320 cm−1 can be related to stretching vibrations (C=O) and (CO) of carboxylic acid groups, respectively. The absorption band at 1223 cm−1 is allocated to the (CO) stretching vibration of choline chloride. The stretching and binding vibrations of (CN) were observed at 1383 cm−1 and 1078 cm−1, respectively. Also, the absence of stretching vibration at about 2550 cm−1 corresponding to the free SH group confirms that the TDES is attached to the surface of cadmium atoms through the sulfur atom. These observations indicate that the CdSe QDs have been successfully capped with TDES.

In order to investigate the crystalline structure of the TDES-CdSe QDs, the XRD pattern was obtained in the range 2θ = 10–70° (Fig. 2B). According to Fig. 2B, the peaks located at 2θ values of 26.4°, 43.8° and 50.6° are attributed to (1 1 1), (2 2 0) and (3 1 1) crystal planes of zinc blended phase of CdSe structure [32,33]. In addition, because of the small crystallite size, these peaks are much broadened. The disappearance of the peaks at 2θ = 35° and 46° assigned to (1 0 2) and (1 0 3) crystal planes, confirming zinc-blended structure. The crystallite size of the TDES-CdSe QDs can be calculated from the full width at half- maximum of the diffraction peak at 2θ of 26.4° by the Scherer equation [34]. An average diameter of TDES-CdSe QDs was obtained to be 5.5 nm.

The TEM image of TDES-CdSe QDs shows a spherical morphology and uniform distribution (Fig. 2C). The particle size histogram (Fig. 2D) of TDES-CdSe QDs spread over the range from 4 to 8 nm with average diameter size of about 6 nm.

To further verify successfully synthesis the TDES-CdSe QDs, the elemental analysis was carried out using EDX analysis. Fig. 2E shows the EDX spectrum of the TDES-CdSe QDs. The EDX microanalysis confirmed that as-prepared QDs was comprised of carbon (C), nitrogen (N), sulfur (S), oxygen (O), chlorine (Cl), cadmium (Cd) and selenium (Se) elements, demonstrating the formation of TDES-CdSe QDs.

To evaluate the kinetics of response of the TDES-CdSe QDs probe to uranyl ions, a solution with a certain concentration (200 nM) of uranyl ions was added to the probe solution and the fluorescence emission spectra were recorded in a period time of 60 min.The results showed that during 5 min, the fluorescence intensity of the probe decreased and the interaction completed within 5 min and then it remained constant (Fig. S2). Therefore, the TDES-CdSe QDs probe can response quickly to the uranyl ions. This significant feature suggests that the TDES-CdSe QDs probe can be applied for fast detection of uranyl ions.

In this study, a FCCCD was applied as a good experimental design strategy for optimizing variables and modeling which allowed us to predict excellent conditions for the detection of uranyl ions using the proposed method. A quadratic polynomial function was predicted by the software to describe the system. The quality of the suggested model was checked by the analysis of variance (ANOVA). Table 3 presents the ANOVA results of the quadratic model for the detection of uranyl ions. The values of F and P were used to understand whether the model is significant or not. In general, a high F value and a low P value < 0.05 are desirable. In this case, the values of F and P were obtained as 29.25 and <0.0001, respectively, indicating the model is significant. Also, the lack of fit (LOF) P value (0.1193) >0.05 (Table 3), meaning the LOF is not significant and the model fits the data well. The data obtained from the experimental design were fitted to a quadratic model by multiple linear regression that describes by the following coded Eq. (1) after backward mode selection:
(1)
ΔF = + 103.01 − 9.90
A − 7.10
B − 26.80 C − 56.06
A 2
– 24.94
B 2
where linear terms (A, B and C) and the quadratics terms (A2 and B2) were important in the detection of uranyl ions. As can be seen, the model equation does not include any interaction terms, because these terms are not significant. The coefficients in the model explain the influence of variables on the analytical response at a confidence limit of 95%. To check the accuracy and sufficiency of the obtained model, the statistical parameters for response like correlation of coefficients (R2, R2 adjusted and R2 predicted) and adequate precision were examined. A high value of R2 (0.9618) demonstrated that the response (a dependent variable) could be successfully described using the independent variables. Further, the high values of predicted R2 (0.9246) is close to adjusted R2 (0.9482), which confirms a good correlation between the predicted and experimental data of analytical response. Adequate precision describes the signal to noise ratio which should be greater than 4. This study shows signal to noise ratio of 26.535, indicating to adequate signal. The selected regression model was examined for normality. According to Fig. 3A, the residues are distributed along a linear line that confirms the normality of the model. Also, the Fig. 3B represents the plot of predicted values versus the actual values, indicating the reasonable efficiency and acceptability of the selected model.

The perturbation plot shows the influences of a main variable on the response by fixing other variables at a reference point. The perturbation plots for the response with respect to sample pH value (A), buffer concentration (B) and TDES-CdSe QDs volume (C) are shown in Fig. 3C. As can be seen, a steep curvature as well as steep slope is observed in the perturbation plot of pH value and volume of TDES-CdSe QDs, indicating that the detection of uranyl ions is very sensitive to the sample pH and TDES-CdSe QDs amount. The relative gentle curvature in the perturbation plot of buffer concentration (B) demonstrates less sensitivity of the response to this factor rather than the pH value (A) or the TDES-CdSe QDs amount (C).

Contour plot is a response surface plot in two dimension (2D) spaces that provides a good understanding of the effect of variables and their interactions on the analytical response. Fig. 3D displays the contour plot of the interaction between the sample pH and the buffer concentration. As can be seen, the response improved with increasing pH from 4.25 to 5.00 and then decreased in the range of 5.00–5.75. Also, according to Fig. 3D, the best response was obtained when buffer concentration was at the minimum concentration (10 mM).

Fig. 4 shows the effect of each variable on the response. pH is the vital factor for the detection of uranyl ions and has a clear effect on the fluorescence emission of TDES-CdSe QDs and present species of uranyl ions. Therefore, the pH effect was examined in the range from 4.25 to 5.75. According to Fig. 4A, the response increased with increasing pH in the range of 4.25–5.00, while it decreased from 5.00 to 5.75. At pH values below 5.0, the response decreased due to dissociation of TDES-CdSe QDs because of protonation of mercapto group binds to the surface of CdSe QDs. Above pH 5.0, the reduced response can be ascribed to the formation of UO2(OH)2 precipitation. Therefore, the maximum response was obtained when the pH was set to 5.0 and selected for subsequent experiments. Also, the effect of buffer concentration on uranyl ions monitoring in the range from 10 to 30 nM was examined (Fig. 4B). It can be seen that the high buffer concentration possesses a negative effect on the response could be arising from the ionic strength effect on fluorescence emission of TDES-CdSe QDs. So, the buffer concentration of 10 mM was selected as optimum amount for further studies. Additionally, the influence of TDES-CdSe QDs volume on uranyl detection was investigated (Fig. 4C). The response improved by increasing the volume of TDES-CdSe QDs in the range of 70 to 90 μL. Therefore, the volume of 90 μL TDES-CdSe QDs is recommended as the optimal value. Higher volumes than 90 μL cannot be used because of reading limitation. Finally, the predicted response by the model at optimal conditions was confirmed by five replicate experiments at optimal conditions. It was observed that the average of experimental response (160) corresponded to the predicted response (155).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the fluorometric probe for uranyl ions
ce:section.ce:para:
The performance of the TDES-CdSe QDs probe was investigated for the sensitive detection of various uranyl - concentrations under the optimal conditions by recording the fluorescence emission spectra. According to Fig. 5A, the fluorescence intensity of TDES-CdSe QDs probe gradually quenched upon addition of uranyl ions from 0 to 1000 nM. The quenching of the fluorescence intensity displayed a good linear relationship with the uranyl ion concentrations in the range of 10–500 nM (Fig. 5B). The limit of detection (LOD) calculated based on 3Sb/m (S
b is the standard deviation of five replicate measurements of the blank and m is the slope of linear range) and found to be 5.7 nM, which is comparable to the most sensitive analytical techniques such as ICP-MS and ICP-OES. Further, to investigate the repeatability and precision of the developed method, the relative standard deviation was tested by performing five replicate measurements of uranyl ions at a concentration level of 100 nM. The RSD% was estimated to be 1.9%. These results demonstrated the efficiency of the assay using TDES-CdSe QDs probe for the uranyl ions determination.

The quenching of fluorescence intensity of TDES-CdSe QDs has been attributed to the binding uranyl ions to the surface of TDES-CdSe QDs, which can be described by a Langmuir-type binding isotherm. According to Langmuir isotherm, the surface of TDES-CdSe QDs includes a finite number of binding sites that each site can attach to one uranyl ion. A relationship between fluorescence intensity and uranyl ion concentration is described by the Eq. (2):
(2)
C
I
=
1 BIₒ
+
C
Iₒ
where I0 and I are fluorescence intensities of TDES-CdSe QDs for the uranyl ion free solution and for a solution with a certain uranyl concentration, respectively, C is the uranyl ion concentration as quencher and B is the binding constant. Fig. 5C displays the dependence of C/I as a function of C. As Fig. 5C shows, the dependence between the [C]/I and [C] is linear throughout the entire uranyl concentration range. Deviation from the linearity or Langmuir behavior at higher concentration has been ascribed to the aggregation of the TDES-CdSe QDs upon addition of uranyl ions [35]. The linearity between the [C]/I and [C] at lower uranyl ion concentration with the correlation coefficient R2 > 0.990 recommends that the binding one ion to each site on the surface of TDES-CdSe QDs is responsible for the quenching.

Fluorescence quenching of TDES-CdSe QDs using metal ions can be explained by five mechanism including the cation-exchange, the electron transfer, ligand competition, binding to surface ligands and electron transfer, and inner filter effect [20]. Some experiments were performed to explain the fluorescence quenching mechanism of TDES-CdSe QDs to uranyl ion. As displayed in Fig. 1B, the maximum fluorescence intensity of TDES-CdSe QDs is observed at 560 nm in the absence and presence of uranyl ions without a red or blue displacement of peak wavelength. It is recommended that the TDES-CdSe QDs fluorescence is suppressed without cation-exchange or electron transfer mechanism. Moreover, according to the Langmuir isotherm plot (Fig. 5C), it is found that uranyl ions are attached to the surface ligand. Based on these results, we suggest that the quenching of fluorescence intensity may be due to the binding of uranyl ions with TDES on the surface of QDs and then transfer of electrons from TDES (donor) to uranyl ions (acceptor), thereby reducing the uranyl ions.

In order to assess the selectivity of the TDES-CdSe QDs probe, a concentration of 200 nM of different cations and anions under optimal conditions was introduced separately to the TDES-CdSe QDs and the corresponding fluorescence emission spectra were recorded. As expected, uranyl ions induced maximum quenching in fluorescence intensity among the investigated cations and anions (Fig. 6A). Evidently, copper ions (Cu2+) displayed a moderate effect on reducing the intensity of fluorescence. To eliminate the effect of Cu2+ on the determination of uranyl ions, thiourea (20 μM) was used as a masking agent. These results indicated that the TDES-CdSe QDs could be considered as a suitable probe for the determination of uranyl ions. Also, the performance of the developed method in the presence of possible interferents was examined. For this purpose, binary solutions including uranyl ions (200 nM) and possible interfering cation or anion at 20 μM concentration level were prepared and introduced separately to the TDES-CdSe QDs probe. Only the concentration of copper ions was fixed at 3 μM (15 times higher than the uranyl concentration). As shown in Fig. 6B, no significant effect was observed on the detection of uranyl ions. These results demonstrate selectivity of the TDES-CdSe QDs probe to uranyl ions in aqueous samples.

To further validate the applicability of this method to determine uranyl ions in practical applications, we applied the suggested method to analysis of uranyl ions in environmental water samples including tap water and sea water. As can be seen from Table 4 , no uranyl ions were detected in the two water samples. Therefore, water samples were spiked with various concentrations of uranyl ions (20, 50, 100, 150 and 200 nM) and tested using the proposed method. According to Table 4, the recoveries of uranyl ions ranged from 97.5% to 105.0%, indicating that matrix effect had no significant effect on the determination of uranyl ions. Also, all RSD% values were below 6%. These results confirm that the developed method can be employed for uranyl ions detection in practical applications with good precision and accuracy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of the proposed method with other fluorometric methods based on QDs for uranyl ions determination
ce:section.ce:para: A comparison between the proposed method and some previously reported fluorometric methods based on QDs for the determination of uranyl ions [16,22–24,36] is given in the Table 5 . The developed method acquires linear range and LOD comparable or better than other methods. In addition, the response time of the probe is much shorter than previously reported.

A single step and green approach has been introduced to synthesis TDES-CdSe QDs in an aqueous solution without using an inert atmosphere. The fluorescence intensity of TDES-CdSe QDs was dramatically decreased upon addition of uranyl ions, whereas other ions have no significant influence on the fluorescence intensity. Therefore, TDES-CdSe QDs can be employed as a ultrasensitive and selective probe for monitoring of uranyl ions. The LOD of the proposed method is considerably less than the maximum acceptable level of uranyl ions in drinking water. The rapid response of the TDES-CdSe QDs probe to uranyl ions candidates it for real-time and on site uranyl monitoring. Other advantages of the suggested method include excellent accuracy and precision, wide linear range, good stability, low cost and use of environmentally friendly materials and reagents. Furthermore, because of high variety of DESs, by selecting the appropriate DES, the fluorescence properties and surface modification of CdSe QDs can be changed. The effectiveness of the proposed method for the determination of uranyl ions in tap and sea water samples was investigated and the desired results were obtained.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors are grateful to the Research Council of the University of Birjand for supporting this work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.molliq.2020.113753.
